# The Book Harness Reference {#sec-appendix-harness}

Every figure in this book was captured by a small pipeline that builds
an instrumented copy of a chapter's example, runs it headless in the
Simulator under scripted input, photographs named frames, and fails the
whole build if the example crashes or stalls. @sec-hello introduced the
two files that ride along in every example; @sec-harness taught the
testing philosophy they implement. This appendix is the third pass: the
complete reference — every function, file, Makefile target, and
protocol step — written so you can lift the pipeline into your own
project. None of it is book-specific; it is the shipped games'
smoke-test harness with a camera attached.

The pipeline has four parts, and they meet at two seams:

1. **`shots.lua`** — per-example: a declarative script of *what to do
   and what to photograph*.
2. **`bookharness.lua`** — shared: the runtime that executes that
   script inside the game.
3. **`example.mk`** — shared Makefile rules that stage and build two
   flavors of the same source.
4. **`tools/shoot.sh`** — the orchestrator that runs one example end
   to end and collects the pictures.

The seams: a generated file (`bookflag.lua`) carries build-time
configuration into the Lua world, and the game's data directory
(`Disk/Data/<bundleID>/`) carries results back out. Nothing else
crosses the boundary — no sockets, no IPC, no Simulator plugins —
which is why the whole thing is portable to any project that can run
`make` and `pkill`.

## The Shots table

Each example's `source/shots.lua` defines one global table. Here is a
complete, annotated example — the schema is four keys, all optional,
though a Shots table with no `shots` and no `script` is just an
expensive way to do nothing:

```lua
Shots = {
    -- RNG seed. The harness calls math.randomseed(seed) at load
    -- time, so every run of this script is bit-identical. Omit it
    -- and figures stop being reproducible on the first math.random.
    seed = 1,

    -- How many frames to run before reporting done. The harness
    -- actually runs to max(last, every frame named in shots), so
    -- this is a floor, not a hard cut.
    last = 390,

    -- The photographs: figure-name -> frame number. The frame is
    -- captured AFTER that frame's update has drawn, and lands in
    -- figures/<example>/<name>.png. Names here and figure
    -- references in the chapter must match exactly.
    shots = {
        ["modes-title"] = 30,
        ["modes-play"]  = 150,
        ["modes-pause"] = 200,
    },

    -- Synthetic input, called once per frame. Whatever table this
    -- returns is handed to the game as this frame's bot input; nil
    -- means "no scripted input this frame". Key names are YOURS --
    -- they mean whatever your Input.gather consumes.
    script = function(frame)
        if frame == 60 then return { confirm = true } end
        if frame > 60 then return Bot.dodge() end   -- read G, decide
    end,
}
```

Two design points carry most of the value. The script speaks the
*game's* input language — the table it returns is the same shape as
the snapshot `Input.gather()` builds from real buttons (@sec-modes), so
the game cannot tell a bot from a thumb. And because everything is
keyed by frame number under a fixed seed and fixed timestep, a shot
frame is a *coordinate*: "frame 200 shows the pause panel" stays true
run after run, which is what lets a book commit its screenshots and
regenerate them only when the code changes.

## bookharness.lua

The shared runtime is 90 lines, printed here in full:

{{< snip _shared/bookharness.lua >}}

The public API is four functions and a flag:

| Member | Role |
|---|---|
| `Harness.enabled` | `SMOKE_BUILD` from the generated `bookflag.lua`. False in release builds, making every other member a no-op. |
| `Harness.input(frame)` | The bot seam. Returns `Shots.script(frame)` in instrumented builds, `nil` otherwise — call it first in your input gathering and fall back to real buttons on `nil`. |
| `Harness.frame(frame, updateFn)` | The wrapper. Runs one frame of the real game inside `pcall`, captures any shots due this frame, and emits heartbeats. Called from `playdate.update`. |
| `Harness.count(key, n)` | Increment a named counter (default 1). Counters ride along in every heartbeat — "count everything" is the telemetry habit of @sec-harness. |
| `Harness.set(key, val)` | Set a counter to an absolute value — for gauges (mode name, boss HP) rather than tallies. |

Reading the listing top to bottom, the load-time block seeds the RNG
and computes `lastNeeded` — the run length — as the larger of
`Shots.last` and the latest named shot, so a typo'd shot frame extends
the run instead of silently never firing. `Harness.frame` then does the
per-frame work, and its details are the accumulated lessons of the
shipped games' harnesses:

- **Unthrottled refresh.** On frame 1 it calls
  `playdate.display.setRefreshRate(0)` — run as fast as the host CPU
  allows. Because all logic is frame-indexed with a fixed `DT`
  (@sec-modes), the run is identical to a 30 fps run, just faster in
  wall-clock: a 390-frame script finishes in a couple of seconds.
- **First error wins.** The update runs inside `pcall`; on failure the
  error is written to the `err` datastore *once* (`errWritten`).
  Later frames usually repeat the same symptom — the first traceback
  is the one that names the cause.
- **Capture after draw.** Shots are taken after `updateFn` returns, so
  the PNG shows exactly what that frame put on screen.
  `playdate.simulator.writeToFile` writes a real host-side PNG (unlike
  `datastore.writeImage`, which writes the device `.pdi` format
  regardless of the extension you ask for).
- **Heartbeats, then done.** Every 90 frames the counters are written
  to the `smoke` datastore with the current frame number; at
  `lastNeeded` the same table goes out with `done = true`. A watching
  script can distinguish "healthy but running," "crashed" (`err.json`
  exists), and "hung" (heartbeat frame stopped advancing).

The wrapper in each example's `main.lua` is the standard two-liner —
and note the import order above it: `import "shots"` must precede
`import "bookharness"`, because the harness reads `Shots` at load time
to seed the RNG before any game code runs:

```lua
local realUpdate = playdate.update
function playdate.update()
    frame = frame + 1
    Harness.frame(frame, realUpdate)
end
```

## example.mk: one source, two builds

Each example's Makefile is two lines — `NAME := Modes` and `include
../../example.mk` — and the shared rules produce two flavors of pdx
from the same source:

| Target | What it builds |
|---|---|
| `make book` | Instrumented build in `build/<slug>/book/`: source staged, `bookharness.lua` copied in, and a **generated** `bookflag.lua` containing `SMOKE_BUILD = true` and `SHOT_PREFIX = "<abs path to figures/<slug>/raw>/"`. This is what `shoot.sh` runs. |
| `make release` | Clean build in `out/<NAME>.pdx` with a generated `SMOKE_BUILD = false` flag — the harness compiles in but does nothing. What a player would get. |
| `make run` | `release`, then opens the pdx in the Simulator (using the 3.0.6-safe launch form of @sec-appendix-setup). |

The generated-file trick is the point to steal. `bookflag.lua` does not
exist in anyone's `source/` directory; the Makefile writes it into the
*staged copy* at build time. That gives the Lua side compile-time
configuration — a boolean and an absolute host path — without a
config file to forget to edit, and it guarantees a release build cannot
accidentally ship with the harness armed, because the release rule
generates the `false` flag unconditionally. The same mechanism carries
the shot directory: `SHOT_PREFIX` bakes the *host* filesystem path for
`playdate.simulator.writeToFile` into the build, which is how shots
land directly in the book's `figures/` tree.

Staging itself is one `cp -r source/* …` — and the `-r` is a scar,
not a habit:

```makefile
# Staging always uses `cp -r`: a bare `cp source/*` silently skips
# subdirectories and pdc will happily build the incomplete bundle.
```

A bare `cp` copies the top-level files, *warns on stderr nobody
reads*, and skips `images/` and `sounds/` — and `pdc` then compiles
the incomplete bundle without complaint, producing a pdx whose failures
(missing images, silent nil sounds) point everywhere except at the
copy. Every staged pipeline in the shipped games hit some version of
this before standardizing on `cp -r`.

## tools/shoot.sh: the protocol

`tools/shoot.sh <example-slug>` runs one example end to end. It is
~60 lines of bash; the steps, in order, with the reasoning:

1. **Derive the coordinates.** From the slug: the bundle ID
   (`com.sdwfrost.book.` + slug with dashes removed), and from it the
   data directory `Disk/Data/<bundleID>/`; the pdx path from `NAME` in
   the example's Makefile; the figure directory `figures/<slug>/`.
   Everything else is mechanical once these are right — and a typo'd
   bundle ID means polling an empty directory forever, the trap
   flagged back in @sec-hello.
2. **Build.** `make book`. A compile error stops the run here, with
   `pdc`'s message.
3. **Kill the Simulator.** `pkill -9 -f "Playdate Simulator"` — it is
   single-instance, and a survivor from the last run would swallow the
   launch.
4. **Wipe state.** Delete the example's data directory and the raw
   figure directory. Stale `smoke.json` or leftover PNGs from a
   previous run would satisfy every check below and lie.
5. **Launch.** `open "$SIM" --args "$PDX"` — explicit app path, no
   `-g`, absolute pdx path: the 3.0.6-safe form
   (@sec-appendix-setup).
6. **Poll.** Every 2 seconds, up to 90: if `err.json` exists, print it
   and **fail** — the game crashed, and the first error is already in
   the file. If `smoke.json` contains `"done":true`, succeed. If the
   loop expires, print the last heartbeat (which says how far the run
   got) and fail as a hang.
7. **Kill again.** Success or failure, no Simulator outlives the run.
8. **Wipe the datastore.** Test data must not leak into real saves
   (@sec-appendix-setup).
9. **Verify the shots.** Every run must produce at least one PNG;
   `shoot.sh` fails loudly if the raw directory is empty — the
   defense against a `Shots.shots` table that quietly stopped matching
   any frame.
10. **Upscale and stamp.** Each raw 400x240 capture is doubled to
    800x480 with `magick -filter point -resize 200%` — nearest
    neighbor, so every screen pixel becomes a crisp 2x2 block, no
    smoothing — then the raw directory is removed and a `.stamp` file
    records success.

The stamp is what the book's top-level `make figures` keys on: an
example is re-shot only if its sources (or the shared harness, or
`shoot.sh` itself) are newer than its stamp, and the whole figures
target runs `.NOTPARALLEL` because the Simulator is single-instance.
Shooting is therefore also the book's continuous smoke test — a
chapter whose example crashes cannot render with stale figures;
the build stops at step 6 instead.

## Adapting it to your project

The pipeline assumes almost nothing. To reuse it:

1. **Route your input through one snapshot function** with the bot
   consulted first (@sec-modes, @sec-input). This is the only change
   to game code, and it is the same seam an autopilot needs
   (@sec-harness) — the camera is a free rider on your test rig.
2. **Copy `bookharness.lua`** (rename freely). If you do not need
   figures, delete the shot block and `SHOT_PREFIX` and you have the
   shipped games' pure smoke harness.
3. **Reproduce the two-build staging** in your build system: stage
   source with `cp -r`, generate the flag file into the staged copy,
   `pdc` from there. Any build tool can do this; the load-bearing part
   is that the flag is *generated*, never hand-edited.
4. **Copy `shoot.sh`** and change three variables at the top — bundle
   ID prefix, SDK path, output directory. Keep the kill/wipe/poll
   ceremony intact; every line of it is a failure mode someone paid
   for.
5. **Write a `Shots` script per thing you want tested or
   photographed.** Start with `last = 300` and no shots: that alone —
   run for ten seconds under a seeded bot without crashing — catches
   a remarkable fraction of regressions.

What you get for the effort is the meta-payoff this book has been
demonstrating on itself: documentation whose screenshots cannot lie,
and a test that plays your game every time you type `make`.
